coredata.dc:title: Crude glycerol as a sustainable source for carbon-based photoluminescent material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Vieira, Amanda T.
Ebert, Hevelyn V.
Therézio, Eralci M.
Terezo, Ailton J.
Barros, Ariane I.
de Siqueira, Adriano B.
Soares, Mario R.S.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon photoluminescent nanoparticles, as carbon quantum dots, have attracted huge attention. This study reports a proposal for the conversion crude glycerol (CG) into carbon-based photoluminescent particles (CPP). Thus, CG was acidified with H3PO4 until to pH 8, 6, 4 and 2, and the glycerol-rich phase (or glycerin acidified (GA)) were submitted to synthesis by microwave assisted method (MW) and hydrothermal treatment (HT). The GA phase from pH 2 (GA2 precursor) provided CPP with: quantum yields were 4.4 % under MW and 13.0 ± 0.9 % by HT; narrow particle size distribution from emission spectra (PL); and elongated particles with onion-like structure (TEM). These results were attributed to the higher purification and PO4 3− concentration reached out in GA2 precursor than GA4 and GA6, as demonstrated by TG/DTA, FTIR, and EGA. Therefore, abundant residual biomass as crude glycerin is an important resource for a higher value-added material (CPP), that it can be applied in agriculture, which attended the Sustainable Development Goals of United Nations, and toward the sustainability of the biodiesel industry, that it is a strategic biofuel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biodiesel
Glycerin
Biomass
Waste valorization
Carbon quantum dots

Acknowledgments are extended to the National Nanotechnology Laboratory (LNNano), part of the National Center for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM), a National Organization supervised by the Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovations (MCTI). The team at the Transmission Electron Microscopy Laboratory is acknowledged for their assistance during the experiments (proposal 20230252). Professor Dr. Fl\u00E1vio Junior Caires and the Laboratory of Thermal Analysis Prof. Dr. Ivo Giolito (LATIG) at the Institute of Chemistry, S\u00E3o Paulo State University \u201CJ\u00FAlio de Mesquita Filho\u201D (UNESP) in Araraquara-SP.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra for GA2, GA4 e GA6 precursors.
Gram-Schmidt curves from gaseous evolved during thermal decomposition GA2, GA4 e GA6 precursors.
FTIR spectra related Gram-Schmidt curves from gaseous evolved during thermal decomposition for a) GA2; b) GA4; and c) GA6 precursors.

a) Photoluminescence emission spectra about scan excitation wavelengths; b) Photoluminescence emission spectra, with excitation at 380 nm, of the carbon-based photoluminescence particles (CPP) synthesized by microwave assisted method (CPP-MW). (Inset: UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the CPP-MW).

a) Integrated photoluminescence emission spectra with excitation at 360 nm, and b) Quantum Yield (QY) of the carbon-based photoluminescence particles (CPP) synthesized by hydrothermal treatment (CPP-HT), both as function of the experimental conditions.
Normalized photoluminescence emission spectra, with excitation at 380 nm, of the CPP-HT as function experimental condition: a) CPPA-HT; b) CPP6-HT; c) CPPA-HT; and d) CPP2-HT.

TEM images of the carbon-based photoluminescence particles (CPP) synthesized by hydrothermal treatment (HT) from GA2 precursor (CPP2-HT) a) high resolution (Inset: line profile analysis); b) low magnification (Inset: EDS spectra).

head.ce:title: Crude glycerol as a sustainable source for carbon-based photoluminescent material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Exact and Earth Sciences, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Brazil.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Science and Technology in Nanotechnology for Sustainable Agriculture, INCTNanoAgro, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Science and Technology in Nanotechnology for Sustainable Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Soils and Rural Engineering, Faculty of Agronomy and Zootechnics, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Mato Grosso, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Soils and Rural Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Soils and Rural Engineering, Faculty of Agronomy and Zootechnics, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Mato Grosso, Brazil.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Chemistry, Federal University of Mato Grosso, Cuiaba, Brazil.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon photoluminescent nanoparticles, as carbon quantum dots, have attracted huge attention. This study reports a proposal for the conversion crude glycerol (CG) into carbon-based photoluminescent particles (CPP). Thus, CG was acidified with H3PO4 until to pH 8, 6, 4 and 2, and the glycerol-rich phase (or glycerin acidified (GA)) were submitted to synthesis by microwave assisted method (MW) and hydrothermal treatment (HT). The GA phase from pH 2 (GA2 precursor) provided CPP with: quantum yields were 4.4 % under MW and 13.0 ± 0.9 % by HT; narrow particle size distribution from emission spectra (PL); and elongated particles with onion-like structure (TEM). These results were attributed to the higher purification and PO4 3− concentration reached out in GA2 precursor than GA4 and GA6, as demonstrated by TG/DTA, FTIR, and EGA. Therefore, abundant residual biomass as crude glycerin is an important resource for a higher value-added material (CPP), that it can be applied in agriculture, which attended the Sustainable Development Goals of United Nations, and toward the sustainability of the biodiesel industry, that it is a strategic biofuel.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) nanoparticles shown important properties as zero-dimensional, quasi-spherical morphology, and fluorescence [1]. These carbon-based photoluminescent particles (CPP) present various applications such as in agriculture [2], due to their facile functionalization, high dispersibility in water, excellent biocompatibility, and synthesis through simple methods using biomass, which promotes sustainability [3]. There are two approaches for the CPP synthesis, that is, top-down and bottom-up [4]. However, in order to following the Green Chemistry Principles and Sustainable Development Goals, the bottom-up approach has been more widely employed [5] as microwave assisted methods [6,7] or hydrothermal treatments [8]. Due the current abundance and increasing biodiesel production, crude glycerol (CG) emerges as advantageous precursor to CPP. Actually, the International Energy Agency (IEA) [9] estimate 5 billion liters of biodiesel from 2024, which two-thirds will be demanded by emergent economies as India, Indonesia, and Brazil.

CPP synthesized by microwave assisted methods have been obtained from glycerol with phosphate salts, and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) [10] or polyethyleneimine (PEI) [11]. The time synthesis were around 15 min, and provided quantum yields (QY) of 12 % and 15 % with TTDDA e PEI, respectively. Functionalizers agents along glycerol have increased QY up to 20 % [12,13], but when it used only glycerol the QY is around 5 % [10,14]. Other methods have been investigated such as reflux system using glycerol and citric acid [15], hydrothermal treatment of glycerol with the functionalizers citric acid and urea [16], and succinic acid [17]. Feng et al. [18] reported a systematic study with N-Dots (nitrogen-rich CQD) obtained from 2-azidoimidazole in glycerol, propanediol, and propanol. The authors showed that higher photoluminescent efficiency (27.9 %) was reached out with glycerol, due higher concentration of hydroxyls groups. It is important clarify that glycerol mentioned here refers to high purity glycerin, therefore glycerin commercial have been used as precursor in these synthesis.

Some methods of carbon quantum dots synthesis provided insight toward to scale up [5,15]. This is a huge opportunity, because there is a large volume of biomass such as crude glycerol, which presents a perspective of increasing to obtain this higher value-added nanomaterial. Therefore, the conversion CG into CPP is important to sustainability biodiesel industry, a strategic biofuel.

The issue about CG lies in its complexity, which results in low purity, high viscosity, and toxicity [19]. There are various purification procedures of CG as use ion exchange resin, nano-cavitation, membrane separation, simple distillation under reduced pressure, adsorption on activated carbon, acidification, and solvent extraction [20]. Acidification process using H3PO4, H2SO4, HCl, and citric acid have provided glycerin purified with low cost [21].

These acids have been similar performances towards to purification CG. However, H3PO4 have showed more advantageous because to provide a shorter time phase separation and presents biogenic nature, which can be used as fertilizers in agriculture [22]. These are important considerations regarding CPP scale production, due to the amount of purified glycerin required and the consequent need for a large volume of strong acid. Thus, if we are thinking about sustainability, Green Chemistry Principles, and environmentally concerns, phosphoric acid emerges as the most promising candidate for CG purification. Consequently, it can be considered a renewable precursor for CPP synthesis through microwave assisted (MW) or hydrothermal treatment (HT).

Crude glycerol (CG) was acquired from Bio Oleo Industria e Comercio de Biocombustivel, Cuiaba, Mato Grosso, Brazil, and it has been founded on solid state due to the complexity sample, which results of the contaminants. The biodiesel production was carried out using the homogeneous transesterification method, with sodium methylate and a mixture as raw material (20 % cottonseed oil, 10 % animal oil, and 70 % soybean oil). The CG characterization is described in the Supplementary Material (Table SM1).

Therefore, it was realized CG solubilization following ratio of 1:1.5 (w/v), so 40 kg CG was dissolved in 60 L of distilled water, thereby reaching out a matrix with pH 10. Under magnetic agitation, H3PO4 83 % (v/v) was added in this matrix until to achieve the pH 8, 6, 4, and 2, respectively. After, the mixtures were transferred to separating funnel and left to rest for 24 h. Thus, the matter organic non-glyceride (MONG) phase, containing free fatty acids (FFA) and esters, as well as the glycerol-rich aqueous phase (or glycerin acidified (GA)) were obtained. The solvent was extracted using an IKA RV 10 Control.

The GA phase had thermal behavior investigated by thermogravimetric and differential thermal analysis (TG/DTA) using a simultaneous thermal analyzer DTG-60H Shimadzu, under 10 °C min−1, and dry air atmosphere with 100 mL min−1.

Fourier Transform Infrared absorption spectra (FTIR) of the GA precursor were obtained by spectrometer Nicolet iS10, using attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory with a Ge crystal, of 4000-600 cm−1 range, under 32 scans, and resolution at 4 cm−1.

The evolved gases analyses (EGA) were performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer TG-DSC 1 from Mettler, coupled with a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer Nicolet iS10. The experiment was conducted in N2 atmosphere, with 60 mL min−1 and heating rate of 20 °C min−1. The transfer line of stainless steel-coated glass, with length of 120 cm, and internal diameter of 2 mm, it was heated to 225 °C. FTIR measurements were conducted with a DTGS detector in a gas cell heated to 250 °C. Both the interferometer and the gas cell compartment were purged with N2. The FTIR spectra were collected of 4000-600 cm−1 range, with 16 scans, and resolution at 4 cm−1.

CPP synthesis were carried out with 10 g GA precursors and high purity glycerin reagent (glycerol P.A. (GPA)). Microwave assisted synthesis (MW) were performed in the Multiwave ECO digester from Anton Paar, the samples were deposited in Teflon reactors and heated to 200 °C, under 30 min. The synthesis conducted using hydrothermal treatment (HT) with conventional heating were performed in a Teflon reactor jacketed with stainless steel. The experiments investigated the temperature (150 °C and 200 °C) and time (6 h and 24 h), with three syntheses carried out for each experimental condition.

UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the CPP has been collected by PerkinElmer UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Lambda 265, under room temperature, and between 600 and 200 nm range. The photoluminescence emission spectra were collected using ISS PC1 photon counting spectrofluorometer with Xenon lamp under 300 Watt, stabilized at 14 A, and 0.5 mm slits in both excitation and emission monochromators. For these characterization, the aqueous solutions were deposited in quartz cuvettes of 10 mm optical path.

The quantum yield (QY) was determined by photoluminescence emission spectrum area of the CPP solutions, and standard solution quinine sulfate as 1 × 10−5 mol L−1 in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4, both under excitation at 360 nm. For this, CPP solutions had adjusted absorbance values to close 0.07 at 360 nm.

JEOL microscope JEM 2100F operated at 200 kV, and equipped with a CCD camera Orius SC600A1, it was used for transmission electron microscopy analysis. Images were collected in bright field mode from the sample deposited over copper grids with ultra-thin holey carbon films, 400 mesh (Ted Pella). Previously, the grids were treated using EasiGlow apparatus (Ted Pella) under 25 mA for 50 s.

The interaction between CG and H3PO4 provide the neutralization of alkaline species, and react with soaps, mono- and di-glycerides, which results in three phases with phosphate salts precipitated, MONG (FFA and esters), and glycerol [23]. In this work, CG matrix treated until pH 8 no presented phase separation, thereby this sample was discontinued. Treatments until pH 6, 4, and 2 resulted in MONG phase, which was discarded, and glycerin acidified (GA) phase, that is, GA6, GA4, and GA2 precursors. No salts precipitate was observed, probably they were dissolved in GA phase due excess water content as solvent. In the Supplementary Material (Fig. SM1), the mixtures in the separating funnel are presented.

In order to investigate the glycerol concentration, TG/DTA measurements were performed (Fig. SM2), and GA6 presented two thermal events with the first from initial temperatures until 150 °C, with mass loss of 75 % (w/w). This event is associated an endothermic peak (DTA), which suggest presence a large amount of water as solvent used in the CG solubilization. In the range between 150 °C and 300 °C, the mass loss of 20 % and endothermic peak it was associated the glycerol evaporation, as well as observed for glycerol P.A. (GPA), with 100 % mass loss and endothermic peak.

As demonstrated in the TG/DTA analysis, there was excess water content as solvent. Thus, a concentration procedure performed. TG/DTA curves about GA6, GA4, and GA2 concentrated are showed in Fig. SM3, and in Table SM2 are summarized the mass loss and each thermal event. In the range between 30 °C to 150 °C has been associated to residual water. The thermal decomposition from second and third steps indicated different mass loss and higher temperature inflection, both correlated with pH of the precursors: GA6 (T = 310 °C, Δm2+3 = 79.0 %) > GA4 (T = 300 °C, Δm2+3 = 71.2 %) > GA2 (T = 270 °C, Δm2+3 = 60.4 %). These properties can be attributed to MONG concentration. The ash content (GA6 = 9.5 % < GA4 = 13.4 % < GA2 = 21.6 %) is related to the inorganic phosphate salts dissolved in the GA phase. These behaviors are comparable to results presented from CG purified with H2SO4 and citric acid [24].

In Fig. 1 showed FTIR spectra for GA6, GA4, and GA2, and in Table SM3 are assigned molecular vibrations to characteristic bands of glycerol (-CH2, CO, COH) and phosphate (-POH and PO). Although there is no significant band shift, but there are slightly differences in relative intensities. The ratio bands between 3332 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1, which are associated the vibrations νOH/δO=POH, it was 4.4 for GA2 and approximately 10.0 for GA4 and GA6. These changes are caused by the chemical environment because MONG in different concentrations, but mainly due phosphate species content, which increasing interactions with glycerol hydroxyls. Thus, the band intensity in the 3700-3000 cm−1 range decrease to GA2 < GA4 < GA6, probably because to the higher interaction of hydrogen bonds between glycerol and phosphate groups, and also to the higher MONG concentration.

TG/DTA and FTIR showed significant differences about CG purified, which supported by EGA analyses. In the Gram-Schmidt curves (Fig. 2 ), GA2 presented well-defined band with maximum for 14 min, while GA4 and GA6 presented this band shifted to around 16 and 17 min, respectively. Additionally, GA4 provided another spread band with maximum for 23 min, and GA6 showed a broader band in the range between 18 and 25 min.

In the FTIR spectra related with Gram-Schmidt curves (Fig. 3 a–c), notice carbon dioxide absorptions (2400 cm−1) for all precursors. For GA2, propenal (C3H4O) signs are presented from 12 min, and disappear for 20 min. For GA4 and GA6 propenal signs arises 16 and 17 min, and disappear around 20 min. The second band in GA4 (23 min) and the broader band in GA6 (between 18 and 25 min) showed since 16 and 17 min CH2 (2945 cm−1 and 2888 cm−1) vibrations, as well as stretching CO-H (1050 cm−1), which related to glycerol characteristics bands. This can be attributed to decomposition about residual mono- and/or di-glycerides presents in the GA4 and GA6 precursors.

Siqueira et al. [25] reported a systematic study about microwave assisted synthesis of CQDs with sewage sludge as precursor. From a design experimental, the authors demonstrated that condition of 200 °C and 30 min provided QY of 2.6 %. Feng et al. [18] reported that at 15 min reached out QY of 15 %, and increased to 27.9 % for 30 min, but QY decreased as increasing the time synthesis. Therefore, these findings present an optimum microwave time to higher photoluminescence intensity, as well as said by Liu et al. [11]. In this regard, we evaluated the microwave assisted synthesis (MW) of CPP with GA6, GA4, GA2 precursors and GPA as 200 °C and 30 min.

The CPPs-MW exhibited pH close to GA precursors. For photoluminescence analysis, firstly it was investigated the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4 a), which was highest intensity at 380 nm. Thus, the CPPs-MW nanomaterials had their emission spectra collected in this excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b). CPPA, the CPP obtained from pure glycerin, exhibited the lowest intensity, indicating lower CPP formation compared to the other samples. The maximum intensity at 450 nm is associated with blue emission range, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 102.8 ± 1.32 nm, 103.3 ± 2.60 nm, and 112.7 ± 2.86 nm to CPP2-MW, CPP4-MW, and CPP6-MW, respectively. This size distribution has been founded in bottom-up approaches [4,12]. Liu et al. [10,11] reported FWHM at 90 nm for CQDs obtained from glycerol P.A. and sodium phosphate by microwave assisted synthesis. The inset in Fig. 4b present UV–Vis absorption spectra, and it is observed a well-defined band at 280 nm and another at 217 nm, which correspond n–π∗ transition of C=O bonds on the surface, and π–π∗ transition of C=C bonds contained in the aromatic structure of the core, respectively [5,18]. The CPPA did not present any clear bands, which corroborates its lower CPP formation.

CPP2-MW exhibited QY was 4.4 %, while CPP4-MW and CPP6-MW presented QY was 3.1 %. In the literature has been indicated that the emissive behavior of CQDs originates from radiative recombination on surface traps, and the concentration of sodium phosphate salt does not significantly affect the photoluminescent characteristics, but may influence the formation rate of carbon-based photoluminescence particles [10,11,14]. Furthermore, the well-defined absorbance band at 280 nm indicated that surface is responsible to the photoluminescence emission than carbogenic core (217 nm).

A second CPP synthesis method were performed by hydrothermal treatment (HT). We conducted a large number of experiments to ensuring the reliability and robustness of the crude glycerol purification process, as well as the CPP synthesis. In Fig. SM4 are presented UV–Vis spectra of CPP-HT. CPPA-HT obtained in experiments 2, 3, and 4, CPP6-HT, CPP4-HT, and CPP2-HT in experiment 4, as well as CPP2-HT in experiment 3, showed a shift in the band at 260 nm, which corresponding a molecular states of n-π∗ and π-π∗transition [5,26]. In Fig. SM5 are showed emission spectra of the CPP-HT, collected with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and it is possible observed similar emissive properties presented by CPP-MW. However, the HT synthesis conditions affected significantly the CPP formation rate. CPPA-HT exhibited the lowest emission intensities, independent of time and temperature conditions. Nevertheless, the temperature increased significantly the formation rate about this luminescent material.

For CPP2-HT, CPP4-HT, and CPP6-HT both variables showed to be quite important. In Fig. 5 a are presented the integrated emission spectra as function of the experimental conditions, and it is noticeable that increase the temperature and time increasing emission intensity, and this effect was more remarkable in CPP2-HT. The QY properties are correlated with UV absorption and PL emission at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Fig. 5b presents the QY results as a function of the experimental conditions, showing the same behavior as observed in Fig. 5a. Additionally, the QY-HT is higher than that obtained through microwave assisted synthesis.

In Fig. 6 are presented emission spectra normalized of HT synthesis, collected with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In Fig. 6a, the normalized spectra of CPPA-HT are presented, and the FWHM values obtained from the Lorentz function were 123.5 ± 2.7 nm, 97.2 ± 1.9 nm, 122.7 ± 2.3 nm, and 113.6 ± 1.7 nm to E1, E2, E3, and E4 experiment, respectively. CPP2-HT (Fig. 6d) showed similar photoluminescent profile, and FWHM about 100 nm, independent of experimental conditions. For CPP4-HT (Fig. 6c), the Experiment 1 (E1 = 150 °C and 6 h) provided the broader particle size distribution, that is, FWHM at 117.2 ± 3.5 nm, while the others experimental conditions (E2, E3, and E4) showed size distributions around 100 nm. However, the synthesis conditions presented significantly influence in the emission behavior for CPP6-HT (Fig. 6b). At 150 °C, when increasing time from 6 h to 24 h decreasing the FWHM at 151.3 ± 5.0 nm to 115.1 ± 3.0 nm. Furthermore, increasing the temperature to 200 °C decreasing size distribution from 115.1 ± 3.0 nm to 105.8 ± 2.2 nm (E3). In that temperature, the differences on size distribution reached out as increasing time, that is, the FWHM is 95.6 ± 1.8 nm (E4). The broader size distribution was attributed to different CPP size and, consequently, different emission properties. This effect can be associated with the formation rate of the CPP, which it is influenced by the purity of the precursor and the phosphate concentration.

It is possible to observe maximus at 450 nm and 520 nm, in all photoluminescence spectra. Wang et al. [14] reported similar behavior and associated the maximum at 520 nm as caused by PO4 3− ions, and as well as with larger molecules such as glucose (C6H12O6) and sucrose (C12H22O11). Additionally, different wavelengths emissions can be related to the surface states and passivation effects. There is also the possibility that emission is associated to the nanoparticle sizes due quantum confinement effect, which occurs in inorganic semiconductor quantum dots [12,27]. However, Simpson et al. [27] reported that the fluorescence emission properties about CPP does not depend on nanoparticle size, in contrast to the inorganic semiconductor quantum dots. Actually, to the best of our knowledge, there is no clarity about the emission mechanism of CPP, in general or specifically CPP from glycerol. In majority, the emissions have been associated with surface state and/or passivation effects [11].

CPP samples from experiment 4 (200 °C and 24 h) presented the highest QY compared to the other experimental conditions (Fig. 5b). The QY value found were 13.0 ± 0.9 % for CPP2-HT, 9.3 ± 0.4 % for CPP4-HT, 7.0 ± 1.3 % and 4.2 ± 1.3 % for CPP6-HT and CPPA-HT, respectively. These is higher than QYs obtained by microwave assisted synthesis. The formation of these photoluminescent particles is started with glycerol dehydration and fragmentation, resulting in propenal, acetaldehyde and formaldehyde. The follow step is carbonization process in the higher temperatures, which provide the decomposition into C-C and C=C, that is, the nucleation and growth CPP core. Carboxylic groups are also generated, but on the surface [4,12]. Beside this, the PO4 3− concentration had a significant effect in experiments 3 and 4. At 200 °C, the formation of these photoluminescent particles increased in the order CPP6-HT < CPP4-HT < CPP2-HT as reported by Liu et al [10,11].

In general, carbon-based photoluminescent nanomaterials are summarized as carbon quantum dots, which presented spherical morphology and sizes between from 1.1 to 18 nm [4,10–14,18,28–35]. Thus, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed with CPP2-HT, in order to investigate these properties. In Fig. SM6 are observed elongated clusters between 315 nm and 160 nm, with smaller structures, also as elongated morphology, but size around 10 nm (Fig. SM6d). These particle sizes and morphology are significantly different from those reported in the literature. Simpson et al. [27] described carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as particles down to 10 nm, and above that there are carbon nanoparticles. Therefore, here we called the carbon nanoparticles of carbon-based photoluminescent particle (CPP), due to the size and morphology presented by CPP2-HT.

In the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 7 a), it is possible to observe an amorphous region at the edge particle and another crystalline region, which onion-like structure. The line profile analysis showed that these layers with distance between 0.8 nm ± 0.3 nm. In Fig. 7b is presented low magnification TEM image, where energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) data was collected. There is high concentration of carbon, but noted other elements such as sodium (Na) and phosphorus (P). These is associated with sodium phosphate salts resulting to the neutralization reaction of alkaline species in the CG matrix with H3PO4, and dissolved in the GA precursors.

Estes et al. [36] reported the glycerol dehydration with sulfuric acid resulting in carbon nanoparticles assemble with size around 220 nm, and various layer morphologies. The assemble with 10–20 nm consisted in crystalline and amorphous structures as onion-like. Furthermore, the authors proposed a mechanism about nanoparticles formation as from glycerol dehydration, generated propenal, which is polymerized in acid environment with continuous glycerol dehydration. Then, the polymerized material reached out from intramolecular condensation, cyclization, and cross-linking steps is carbonized, resulting in the carbon-based photoluminescent nanoparticles. A similar mechanism is reported by Hazarika and Karak [37], when treated glycerol with citric acid.

This study focused on converting abundant biomass into carbon-based photoluminescent particles (CPP). The treatment of crude glycerol (pH 10) with H3PO4 until pH 6, 4, and 2, providing glycerol-rich (or glycerin acidified) phase (GA6, GA4, GA2 precursors) with different purities as content MONG (GA6 > GA4 > GA2) and ash (GA2 > GA4 > GA6). However, all precursors were converted into CPP by microwave assisted synthesis (MW) and hydrothermal treatment (HT), but the last one provided higher QY (13 %) with CPP2-HT. The photoluminescence mechanism should be attributed to functional groups on surface.

This proposal could provide greater sustainability in the biodiesel industry, which a highly demanded biofuel aimed it is diversifying the energy matrix, particularly in emergent countries like India, Indonesia, and Brazil. Additionally, it aligns with the green chemistry principles and the Sustainable Development Goals of the United Nations.

Amanda T. Vieira: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Hevelyn V. Ebert: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Eralci M. Therézio: Resources. Ailton J. Terezo: Resources, Conceptualization. Ariane I. Barros: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Adriano B. de Siqueira: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mario R.S. Soares: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Acknowledgments are extended to the National Nanotechnology Laboratory (LNNano), part of the National Center for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM), a National Organization supervised by the Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovations (MCTI). The team at the Transmission Electron Microscopy Laboratory is acknowledged for their assistance during the experiments (proposal 20230252).

Professor Dr. Flávio Junior Caires and the Laboratory of Thermal Analysis Prof. Dr. Ivo Giolito (LATIG) at the Institute of Chemistry, São Paulo State University "Júlio de Mesquita Filho" (UNESP) in Araraquara-SP.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biombioe.2024.107556.
